title: Colonoscopy - ASA III CHF, Sensitive
description: High-complexity colonoscopy sedation for an ASA III patient with CHF, reduced ejection fraction, and elevated PK/PD sensitivity. Emphasizes cautious dose titration, hemodynamic monitoring, and early complication management.
learningObjectives: Recognise how CHF alters drug distribution and sensitivity
Apply conservative dosing strategies for ASA III patients
Detect and manage hypotension in a patient with reduced cardiac reserve
Differentiate hemodynamic compromise from simple respiratory depression
Document key interventions for medicolegal and quality-improvement purposes

states.prompt: Your patient is a 68-year-old male with CHF (EF 35%), NYHA Class II, on metoprolol, furosemide, and lisinopril. Mild dyspnoea on exertion. Baseline BP 108/62, HR 58, SpO2 95% on room air. Airway: Mallampati II, limited jaw opening. Presenting for surveillance colonoscopy. What ASA class would you assign?
explanations: Incorrect — this patient has significant systemic disease.
Incorrect — CHF with reduced EF and NYHA Class II symptoms is more than mild disease.
Correct! CHF with EF 35% and NYHA Class II is ASA III — severe systemic disease without constant threat to life.
ASA IV is reserved for a constant threat to life. This patient is functional at rest.

states.prompt: CHF with reduced ejection fraction alters drug pharmacokinetics. Which of the following BEST describes why this patient requires lower initial benzodiazepine doses?

options.label: Reduced volume of distribution → higher peak plasma concentrations
Increased renal clearance → faster drug elimination
Increased plasma protein binding → more free drug available
Increased GI absorption → higher bioavailability of oral pre-meds

explanations: Correct! Reduced cardiac output decreases Vd for lipophilic drugs, producing higher peak concentrations from a given IV dose.
Incorrect — renal clearance is typically reduced or unchanged; hepatic clearance is reduced due to low cardiac output.
Incorrect — protein binding is often reduced in CHF (hypoalbuminemia), which can actually increase free drug, but the dominant PK effect is reduced Vd.
Incorrect — this scenario concerns IV drugs; GI absorption is irrelevant here.

checklistItems.description: Correctly explains PK reason for dose reduction in CHF
checklistItems.evaluationHint: Patient selected reduced volume of distribution option

states.prompt: It is time to begin sedation. Given the patient's CHF and baseline BP 108/62, what initial midazolam dose would be most appropriate?
explanations: Correct! Start low (0.5 mg) in ASA III/CHF patients — their PK sensitivity means even low doses can overshoot.
1 mg is appropriate for healthy adults but may produce over-sedation and hypotension in this CHF patient.
2 mg is excessive and risks hemodynamic collapse in a patient with borderline BP.
0.25 mg is subtherapeutic in most adults; 0.5 mg is the better conservative starting point.

checklistItems.description: Chose conservative midazolam dose (0.5 mg) for CHF patient

states.prompt: Mild sedation achieved. The endoscope is being introduced — the patient grimaces. What fentanyl dose is appropriate given his CHF and borderline BP?

options.label: Hold — no opioid in CHF patients

explanations: Correct! 25 mcg is the conservative and appropriate starting dose for an opioid-naïve CHF patient.
50 mcg risks significant respiratory depression and vasodilation in a borderline-BP patient.
100 mcg is dangerously high for an opioid-naïve ASA III patient with reduced cardiac reserve.
Opioids are not contraindicated in CHF — adequate analgesia prevents sympathetic surges that worsen hemodynamics.

checklistItems.description: Chose conservative fentanyl dose (25 mcg) for CHF patient

states.prompt:
Sedation achieved. The colonoscopy is underway. Continue close hemodynamic monitoring.

📚 Teaching Point: In patients with CHF, even mild hypotension (MAP < 60 mmHg) can precipitate cardiogenic shock. Trending BP is as important as SpO2 monitoring.
ALERT: BP has dropped to 82/50 (MAP 61 mmHg). HR is 52 (metoprolol effect). SpO2 is 93%. The patient is drowsy but arousable. What is your PRIORITY intervention?

options.label: Give a 250 mL isotonic fluid bolus and reduce FiO2
Give 250–500 mL isotonic fluid bolus AND increase FiO2 to 40%

explanations: Fluid bolus is correct, but reducing FiO2 when SpO2 is 93% is wrong — supplemental O2 should be increased.
Correct! Fluid challenge addresses sedation-induced vasodilation. Increasing FiO2 addresses the concurrent mild hypoxaemia.
Epinephrine is for anaphylaxis or cardiac arrest — this is hemodynamic sedation-related hypotension.
Naloxone may reverse analgesia and trigger sympathetic surge; it is not indicated when hemodynamic compromise is from vasodilation.

checklistItems.description: Correctly managed sedation-induced hypotension with fluid + O2

states.prompt: The procedure is complete. BP is recovering to 100/62. SpO2 is 96% on 4 L/min NC. The patient is responsive but drowsy. Which parameter is MOST important to trend before considering discharge to PACU?

options.label: Trend BP every 5 min — ensure MAP ≥ 70 mmHg for 15 min
MOASS ≥ 3 only — BP is less important in recovery

explanations: Correct! Hemodynamic stability (sustained MAP ≥ 70 mmHg) is the critical discharge criterion for a CHF patient.
Temperature is a valid discharge criterion but not the primary concern here — BP stability is.
Urine output monitoring is appropriate for prolonged procedures, not brief endoscopy recovery.
MOASS ≥ 3 is necessary but not sufficient — hemodynamic stability is equally critical for this ASA III patient.

checklistItems.description: Correctly prioritises BP trending as primary PACU discharge criterion

states.prompt: Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.
